titleStmt.title: A Concise Anglo-Saxon Dictionary — TEI Lex-0 electronic edition
CAS2TEI

respStmt.resp: Digital transcription of the print source
respStmt.orgName: Project Gutenberg, the Germanic Lexicon Project, and the Online Distributed Proofreading Team

availability.licence: Copyright © 2026 Sergei Stoliarov. All rights reserved. No reuse, modification, or redistribution is permitted without prior written permission from the copyright holder.

monogr.title: A Concise Anglo-Saxon Dictionary for the Use of Students

biblStruct.note: Printed at the University Press, Cambridge (John Clay, M.A.). Digital transcription via Project Gutenberg (eBook #31543), produced by Louise Hope, Zoran Stefanovic, the Germanic Lexicon Project, and the Online Distributed Proofreading Team.
As an attestation siglum, 'A' followed by numerals = Anglia; 'A' alone = the Anglian dialect (see langUsage). Overloaded siglum — confirm intended target.

monogr.title: Herrig's Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen
Beiträge zur Geschichte der deutschen Sprache
Publications of the Modern Language Association of America
Transactions of the Royal Society of Literature

biblStruct.note: Author/corpus. References followed by numerals in parentheses refer to certain Homilies attributed to Ælfric in HL. If followed by a book of the Bible, the reference is to that book in 'Ælfric de vetere et novo Testamento' (Bibl. der ags. Prosa, vol. 1).
Quoted by volume, page and line.
edition has a partial glossary or word-index
Poem. Also ed. G. P. Krapp, Boston, 1905 (with referenced glossary).
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
In J. W. Bright's Anglo-Saxon Reader, London, 1892.
edition has a complete glossary, but without references
Poem. Also included with Andreas in Krapp's edition (see An).

monogr.title: King Alfred's version of Augustine's Soliloquies

biblStruct.note: edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
Poem. Also ed. A. J. Wyatt and R. W. Chambers, Cambridge, 1914; ed. W. J. Sedgefield, Manchester, 1912; or ed. Harrison and Sharp, Boston, 1888.
2 vols. Reference usually to pages in vol. 1 for the readings recorded in vol. 2.

monogr.title: Texte und Untersuchungen zur ae. Literatur

biblStruct.note: edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
With the Metres of Boethius (see Met).
edition has a partial glossary or word-index
Mémoires de la Soc. néo-philologique à Helsingfors, tom. 5.
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
Poem; in Gr or in the poetical passages of Chr (†Chr).
Reference dictionary cited within Clark Hall.
Reference dictionary (Supplement) cited within Clark Hall.
7th ed.; or J. F. Davis' translation, London, 1891. Reference dictionary.

monogr.title: A New English Dictionary on Historical Principles

biblStruct.note: The New English Dictionary (now the OED); ed. Sir J. A. H. Murray and others. See Preface and Note 1. For W and parts not yet in NED, reference is to its abridgement, The Concise Oxford Dictionary.
Reference dictionary cited within Clark Hall.
Reference dictionary cited within Clark Hall.
Transcriber's note: occurs a few times in error for the author's normal form, KC.

monogr.title: Charters, wills and similar documents (cover symbol)

biblStruct.note: Collective siglum: documents contained in BC, CC, EC, KC and TC.

monogr.title: Land Charters and other Saxonic Documents

biblStruct.note: edition has a partial glossary or word-index
edition has a partial glossary or word-index

monogr.title: Two of the Saxon Chronicles Parallel

biblStruct.note: edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index

monogr.title: King Alfred's translation of Gregory's Pastoral Care

biblStruct.note: In OET, or in WW (cols. 1–54), or (if numbers are followed by a letter) in A Latin-Anglo-Saxon Glossary, ed. J. H. Hessels, Cambridge, 1890 (referenced glossary).
Grein's Sprachschatz, rev. Köhler and Holthausen; a complete referenced glossary to Gr.
In Germania, Vierteljahrsschrift für deutsche Altertumskunde, vol. 11 (ns).
Rev. R. P. Wülker. The principal anthology of Anglo-Saxon poetry; many poem sigla are 'in Gr'. See GK for a complete referenced glossary.
edition has a partial glossary or word-index
Bibl. der ags. Prosa, vol. 7. CHy = Cambridge Hymns in the same vol.
EHy = Hymns in the same vol.
AF vol. 23. JHy = the Hymns in the same vol.
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
Acta Soc. Sc. Fennicae, vol. 35. LHy = the Hymns in the same vol.
Prose portion (Psalms 1–50) also ed. Bright and Ramsay, Boston, 1907; the verse remainder is in Gr.
Studien zur eng. Philologie, vol. 18. RHy = the Hymns in the same vol.
Stowe MS, but includes marginal readings from APs, CPs and EPs.
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
As contained in OET. VHy = Hymns at the end of the Psalter. Glossary also by Conrad Grimm (AF vol. 18), Heidelberg, 1906.

Collective siglum: any one or more of the Anglo-Saxon Psalters — A(=APs), B(=BPs), C(=CPs), E(=EPs), J(=JPs), L(=LPs), R(=RPs), S(=SPs) and VPs; also Hy. In Psalm numbering the Authorised Version is usually one ahead of the MSS. The occasional form 'Pss' was retained.
The 'Hymns' at the end of most ags. versions of the Psalms. Verse-numbering usually follows Wildhagen's Cambridge Psalter (CPs).
Poem. Also ed. T. W. Hunt (Exodus and Daniel), Boston, 1885. Sometimes written 'Dan' in body text.
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index

monogr.title: Be Dōmes Dæge (from the Exeter Book)

biblStruct.note: Poem; in Gr, or ed. Kent, Boston, 1889.
Poem; in Gr or in Hunt's edition (see Da). If followed by two kinds of numerals = Exodus in 'Ælfric de vetere et novo Testamento' (Bibl. der ags. Prosa, vol. 1, 1872).
Poem; in Gr, and most editions of Beowulf.
Poem. If followed by two kinds of numerals = Genesis in 'Ælfric de vetere et novo testamento' (Bibl. der ags. Prosa, vol. 1, 1872).
Poem. GnE = those in the Exeter Book; GnC = those in the Cotton MS. Separate ed. by B. C. Williams, New York, 1914 (referenced glossary).
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
Poem. Also ed. A. S. Cook, Boston, 1889 (referenced glossary).
Poem; in Gr, also in Br, Kl or Sweet's Anglo-Saxon Reader, Oxford.

monogr.title: The Riddles of the Exeter Book

biblStruct.note: edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
Poem(s); in Gr, or ed. F. Tupper Junr., Boston, 1910 (referenced glossary).
Poem (transcriber's listing). Included in Gr with other texts from its source, the Exeter MS.
Poem; in Gr. If in italics, the reference is sometimes to the prose version, ed. J. M. Kemble.
Poem; in Gr, or ed. R. F. Chambers, Cambridge, 1912.
Surtees Society, vol. 23. Glossary by H. W. Chapman, Yale Studies No. 24, Boston, 1905.
Lines of Anglo-Saxon only counted. Glossary by Uno Lindelöf, Bonn, 1901 (BB vol. 9).
pp. 8–98; or ed. P. Gonser (AF vol. 27), Heidelberg, 1909 (pp. 100–176).

monogr.title: Glosses in (Haupt's) Zeitschrift für deutsches Altertum

biblStruct.note: edition has a partial glossary or word-index
Bibl. der ags. Prosa, vol. 3. See also Æ and Shr.
In Techmer's Internationale Zeitschrift für allgemeine Sprachwissenschaft, vol. 2.

monogr.title: Kentish Glosses to the Proverbs of Solomon

biblStruct.note: = WW 55–88, or, if quoted by number, in Kl.
edition has a complete glossary, but without references

monogr.title: Leechdoms, Wortcunning and Starcraft of the Anglo-Saxons

biblStruct.note: edition has a partial glossary or word-index
3 vols., Rolls Series. Vol. 2 and pp. 1–80 of vol. 3 cited by MS folio, so references may also serve G. Leonhardi's ed. (Bibl. der ags. Prosa, vol. 6).
Transcriber's listing; used only in the first edition.
In Skeat's ed. of the Anglo-Saxon Gospels (see G). Glossary by A. S. Cook, Halle, 1894. LRG = Lindisfarne and Rushworth Gospels (see RG).
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index

As contained in Liebermann, Schmid or Thorpe. Numerals not (or only partly) in parentheses = 'Die Gesetze der Angelsachsen', Liebermann, 2 vols.; numerals entirely in parentheses = vol. 2 of 'Ancient Laws and Institutes', B. Thorpe, 1840. Lieb. = F. Liebermann.
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
In Kleinere angelsächsische Denkmäler (Bibl. der ags. Prosa, vol. 6).
Transactions of the Philological Society for 1907–10, pp. 180–188.
See Preface (place-name compounds in charters; cf. BC and EC).
In the Philological Society's Transactions for 1903–1906 (mostly late texts).
In Hept; or in MLA 13·456–541 (references to passages are always to the latter).
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index
edition supplied with a complete referenced glossary or word-index

monogr.title: Quellen u. Forschungen zur Geschichte der teutschen Lit. u. Sprache

biblStruct.note: edition has a partial glossary or word-index

monogr.title: The Anglo-Saxon and Latin Rule of St Benet (Interlinear Glosses)

biblStruct.note: edition has a partial glossary or word-index
Cols. 1–54 = Cp; 55–88 = KGl; pp. 89–103 = Colloq. Monast. in NED.

monogr.title: Die Syntax in den Werken Alfreds

biblStruct.note: Copious material and word-indexes to AO, BH, Bo, CP, AS, PPs, etc.
See also LG, NG, RG, WG. Glossary to WG by M. A. Harris, Yale Studies vol. 6, Boston, 1899.
Contained in Skeat's edition (see G, LG, RG).

In Skeat's ed. (see G). Mt (all), Mk 1–2^15 and Jn 18^1–3 are Mercian (known as R^1); the rest (R^2) is Northumbrian. Glossary to R^1 by Ernst Schulte, Bonn, 1904; to R^2 by U. Lindelöf, Helsingfors, 1897.
See G and NG. MtL = Lindisfarne MS; MtR = Rushworth MS.
See G and NG. MkL = Lindisfarne MS; MkR = Rushworth MS.
See G and NG. LkL = Lindisfarne MS; LkR = Rushworth MS.
See G and NG. JnL = Lindisfarne MS; JnR = Rushworth MS.
The Mercian part of the Rushworth Gospels: Mt (all), Mk 1–2^15, Jn 18^1–3.
The Northumbrian part of the Rushworth Gospels (all except R^1).
Comprehensive sign for all or any of the extant Anglo-Saxon glosses or glossaries: Cp, Ep, Erf, GPH, HGl, KGl, Ln, OEG, WW, etc.
Sense (I): Wulfstan's Homilies (this work; glossary by L. H. Dodd, New York, 1908). Sense (II): West Saxon (dialect; see langUsage). Overloaded siglum — confirm intended target.
= F. Liebermann; in practice a reference to his edition of the Laws (see LL).

projectDesc.p: TEI Lex-0 encoding of the second edition (1916) of J. R. Clark Hall’s A Concise Anglo-Saxon Dictionary, derived from the Project Gutenberg transcription (eBook #31543). The object language is Old English; the metalanguage of the definitions is Modern English.

editorialDecl.p: Note 1 (references): where references are in italic type, quotations from the texts will be found in the New English Dictionary, under the English word distinguished in the article by quotation marks. In references to special passages, volumes are marked off from pages by an inverted full stop, and lines or verses follow other numerals as small superior figures; where lines are not given, the mark ´ shows the quotation is in the lower half of a page.

Note 2 (glossary indicators): after an edition of a text, (1) indicates a complete referenced glossary or word-index, (2) a complete glossary without references, and (3) a partial glossary or word-index. These are recorded on each source as a note carrying type="glossary".

Note 3 (combination): some abbreviations are used in combination, e.g. MtLR = the Lindisfarne and Rushworth MSS of St Matthew; BJPs = the Bosworth and Junius Psalters; asf. = accusative singular feminine; EK = Early Kentish. The eight gospel×manuscript combinations and R¹/R²/LRG are encoded as separate targetable biblStruct entries; other combinations (e.g. BJPs, MtLR, the Psalter Hymn sigla CHy/EHy/JHy/LHy/RHy/VHy, and GnE/GnC) are formed productively from their components and may be added on the same pattern.
Editorial signs and symbols used in the dictionary:

list.item: ‘ ’ — Quotation marks enclose the English words to be looked up in the NED for etymology and examples (see Note 1). If they enclose Latin words, they indicate the lemmata of Anglo-Saxon words in glosses or glossaries, or the Latin equivalent in the Latin source texts.
* — Prefixed or affixed to hypothetical forms. Normalised forms that actually exist are not usually so marked.
´ — Shows (where lines are not given) that the quotation is in the lower half of a page (see Note 1).
± — The Anglo-Saxon word to which it is prefixed is found both with and without the prefix ge-.
‡ — Occurs in a poetical text, and once only.
== — The word it follows, and its compounds, are found in the Dictionary under the heading given after it (e.g. meht==miht means meht = miht and meht- = miht-).

editorialDecl.p: Grammatical abbreviations. Case/number/gender abbreviations combine systematically (e.g. dp. = dative plural, asf. = accusative singular feminine):

list.item: n. = nominative, or neuter, or note

editorialDecl.p: Abbreviations for languages cited in etymologies (BCP 47 codes given for reference; promote these into the langUsage element if you tag etymons with xml:lang):

list.item: OF. = Old French (also written OFr.) (BCP 47: fro)

cit.quote: to furnish with a bit or bridle, saddle, curb.
to need, have need of, want,

sense.lbl: used as auxiliary to indicate past tense,

cit.quote: to lighten, illuminate, give light, shine,
frame, make, do, perpetrate, commit, afford,
to raise a mound to, bury, inter,
'though,' although, even if, that, however, nevertheless, yet, still,

sense.cit:
any, all, every, each (one), Æ,
AO,
CP

cit.quote: way or path to the funeral pile,
what is taken or separated from,
to ride, run to, reach, gain by running or riding,
